cataloging_subject.label: Zwei mittelenglische Christmas Carols
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is Karl Breul's complete 1890 scholarly article: two independent anonymous Middle English carols, their Latin refrains and alternating lines, and Breul's substantial German introductions, textual commentary, comparisons, source statement, and editorial-practice statement. It is not one medieval work, the whole journal volume, either manuscript as a physical object, the University of Michigan encoding, or the checked-in XML artifact. The two carols and their manuscript traditions remain separately scoped parts.

catalog_summary.title: Zwei mittelenglische Christmas Carols
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous poets; article by Karl Breul
catalog_summary.date: Medieval carols probably fifteenth century; earliest surviving witnesses early to mid-fifteenth century; both first printed in 1843; Breul article published 1890
catalog_summary.region: Original dialect and region unlocalized; Breul notes northern features in the Annunciation carol, while the shared NLS witness was copied in a North-Midland context

assertion.notes: This English display title translates Breul's German heading; it is not a second historical title-page form.
Breul's supplied German section title, not a manuscript rubric.
DIMEV's record name and the poem's repeated Latin refrain.

titles.value: Ecce ancilla domini / Seyd þo virgyn wythowtyn vice

scope.description: Authorship of the modern article prose, framing, and signed source/editorial statement; not authorship of either medieval carol
Breul says he transcribed both poems directly from the Advocates' Library manuscript in 1882.

assertion.notes: Breul's broad dating is retained as a work estimate, separate from the dates of the early-fifteenth-century Cambridge and c.1480 NLS witnesses.
Breul's broad dating is retained as a work estimate, not inferred from one surviving copy.
CUL Add. 5943 preserves a four-stanza version at f.182v; the exact manuscript dating varies by cataloging source.

date_statements.display: Fifteenth century; earliest securely narrow witness range 1430–1444

assertion.notes: Egerton MS 3307 preserves a five-stanza musical version with a variant four-line burden and is securely dated 1430–1444. Bodley 77 is independently cataloged only as fifteenth century, so its binding-strip fragment could be earlier or contemporary; the broadened normalization avoids asserting that Egerton is certainly the first surviving copy.

scope.description: The NLS versions of both carols

assertion.notes: Turnbull's volume prints the Nativity/Epiphany carol on pp.139–140 and the Annunciation carol on pp.141–143. It is the earliest publication listed for either work in DIMEV v1.0.
Breul dates his signed article Cambridge, August 1888; the journal volume was published in 1890.

places.label: Northern England
notes: A cautious linguistic association for features in the Annunciation carol, not a demonstrated place of composition or the NLS copying region.

assertion.notes: Breul identifies several northern spellings or inflectional features but explicitly says the original dialect cannot be determined from the rhymes.

scope.description: The Annunciation refrain, the alternating lines and burden of the Nativity/Epiphany carol, biblical citations, and comparative quotations

form_statements.description: The complete encoded article combines two verse works with substantial German prose commentary and embedded verse quotations.
The first carol has seven eight-line stanzas; the second has a two-line burden and nine quatrains.

assertion.notes: The XML places a two-line Chaucer epigraph inside the second Annunciation LG, so its raw 58 L elements exceed the 56 medieval poem lines.

scope.description: Breul's transcriptions, textual notes, editorial interventions, and commentary

subject_statements.label: Virgin Mary and the angel Gabriel

summaries.value: Karl Breul's complete 1890 article edits and comments on two anonymous fifteenth-century religious lyrics from the Heege Manuscript. Ecce ancilla domini retells the Annunciation in seven eight-line stanzas, closely following Luke 1:26–38 and ending each stanza with Mary's Latin response. As Holy Kirk Makes Mind is a macaronic Nativity and Epiphany carol whose two-line burden and nine quatrains alternate Middle English with Latin and culminate in the Magi and the symbolic meanings of their gifts.

CME00121 encodes the complete printed article on pp.401–408, including both poem texts, Breul's German commentary and comparative verse quotations, the final manuscript and editorial-practice statement, and the Cambridge, August 1888 dateline and signature. It does not encode the rest of Englische Studien volume 14.

The raw XML contains 136 L elements. Ninety-six are descendants of the two poem DIV2 elements, but two of those ninety-six are a Chaucer epigraph placed inside the second Annunciation LG rather than medieval carol lines. The direct edited carol total is therefore 94 lines: 56 for Ecce ancilla domini and 38 for As Holy Kirk Makes Mind. A further 40 L elements belong to verse quotations embedded in Breul's commentary.

Breul says he retained the manuscript's variable spelling but capitalized proper names and line openings, separated u from v, and joined compounds; every departure from the manuscript reading was printed in italics. CME00121 retains this typography as HI/@REND='italic', so italics indicate editorial difference, not manuscript emphasis. The file contains 129 italic HI elements, 96 within the poem divisions and 33 in commentary.

extent.description: One DIV1 contains four DIV2 sections: two poem sections and two commentary sections. The verified textual total is 134 verse lines: 94 direct medieval carol lines and 40 verse-quotation lines in the commentary. The XML has 136 raw L elements because it embeds a two-line modern Chaucer epigraph inside the second Annunciation LG. The commentary sections additionally have 32 P, four Q, and five nested LG. Across the complete article are 12 NOTE, nine PB, 18 MILESTONE, 129 italic HI, one CLOSER, one DATELINE, and one SIGNED.

content_parts.label: Ecce ancilla domini (Mariae Verkündigung)
content_parts.summary: A seven-stanza Annunciation song in which Gabriel announces the Incarnation and Mary repeatedly responds 'Ecce ancilla domini'; Breul aligns its central stanzas with Luke 1:26–38.

locators.value: DIV2 N='1' TYPE='poem'; seven LG; 58 raw L

notes: The medieval poem has 56 lines. The XML's raw 58-L total includes a two-line Chaucer epigraph inserted inside LG N='2'; direct inspection of the print confirms that the two lines are editorial epigraph material, not part of the stanza.
Breul's seven footnotes align stanzas I–VI with Luke; one further textual footnote occurs at line 28.

content_parts.label: As Holy Kirk Makes Mind / Intravit ventris thalamum
content_parts.summary: A macaronic Nativity and Epiphany carol with a two-line burden and nine alternating Middle English and Latin quatrains on the Incarnation, Mary, Christ's birth, the star, and the gifts of the Magi.

locators.value: DIV2 N='2' TYPE='poem'; ten LG; 38 L
ff.65v–66; five stanzas and variant four-line burden

notes: Breul historically locates this carol at fol.59vo ff., but the current NLS images and DIMEV place the complete NLS version on f.59r–59v. The attributed historical statement is preserved rather than silently normalized.
DIMEV's Turnbull bibliography gives pages '13-40'; direct inspection of the 1843 scan confirms pp.139–140 and shows that the DIMEV value dropped a 9.

content_parts.label: Breul's commentary on Mariae Verkündigung
content_parts.summary: German commentary on the carol's relation to Luke, stanza form, repeated formulas, alliteration, vocabulary, northern linguistic features, textual cruxes, and the image of the knotted bond, including a five-stanza comparative quotation.

locators.value: DIV2 N='1' TYPE='commentary'; 11 P, one Q, five nested LG, 22 quoted L

content_parts.label: Breul's commentary on Auf Weihnachten und Epiphanias
content_parts.summary: German commentary on macaronic religious song, the poem's Latin phrases and analogues, textual cruxes, the Magi's gifts, the manuscript source and loci, Breul's 1882 transcription, and his normalization and italic-emendation practices.

locators.value: DIV2 N='2' TYPE='commentary'; 21 P, three Q, 18 quoted L, CLOSER, DATELINE, and SIGNED

related_works.title: The Miller's Tale
notes: Breul inserts two epigraph lines referring to Angelus ad virginem inside the printed Annunciation text; this is a modern editorial comparison, not a source or witness relationship for the carol.

evidence.citation: CME00121 XML source in the pinned CME submodule, structurally and textually inspected 2026-07-13.
evidence.locator: complete file; especially four DIV2 sections and final source/editorial statement
evidence.summary: Confirms the complete article scope, headings, poem and commentary boundaries, text, editorial notes, page references, dateline, signature, source statement, editorial practices, and raw XML metrics.

evidence.citation: XPath element audit of CME00121 at pinned CME commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c, 2026-07-13.
evidence.locator: DIV1, DIV2, P, Q, LG, L, NOTE, PB, MILESTONE, HI, CLOSER, DATELINE, and SIGNED counts
evidence.summary: Computed one DIV1, four DIV2, 32 P, four Q, 22 LG, 136 L, 12 NOTE, nine PB, 18 MILESTONE, and 129 italic HI. The two poem DIV2 elements contain 96 L, including a two-line Chaucer epigraph; commentary contains 40 quoted L.

evidence.citation: Karl Breul, 'Zwei mittelenglische Christmas Carols,' Englische Studien 14 (1890): 401–408.
evidence.summary: Supports the article title and publication, complete scope, poem texts and headings, biblical and comparative commentary, manuscript source and historical loci, 1882 transcription statement, broad dating and dialect cautions, and editorial practices.

evidence.citation: William Barclay David Donald Turnbull, ed., The Visions of Tundale (Edinburgh, 1843), 139–143.
evidence.summary: Supports the earlier independent publication of both NLS carol versions, the historical manuscript designation Jac. V.7.27, and exact printed page ranges.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse, version 1.0, records 1173 and 589.
evidence.summary: Supports work identities, IMEV/NIMEV numbers, genres, subjects, forms, incipits, known witnesses and loci, variant extents, and edition references.

evidence.citation: National Library of Scotland Adv.MS.19.3.1 catalog, IIIF manifest, and direct visual boundary inspection.
evidence.summary: Supports the shared NLS witness identity, c.1480 North-Midland context, current loci, and exact facsimile boundaries: Nativity/Epiphany at canvases 133–134 and Annunciation at canvases 194–196.

evidence.citation: DIMEV v1.0 witness wit-1173-1 and DIAMM source 328 for Cambridge University Library MS Add. 5943.
evidence.summary: Supports the Cambridge witness at f.182v, its four-stanza Annunciation version, early-fifteenth-century context, and current holding.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries catalog for MS. Bodl. 77 and DIMEV v1.0 witness wit-589-1.
evidence.summary: Supports the composite fifteenth-century English paper manuscript and DIMEV's identification of a fragmentary carol version on binding strips at ff.1v and 2.

evidence.citation: British Library catalog records for Egerton MS 3307 and Harley MS 275, with DIMEV v1.0 witnesses wit-589-2 and wit-589-3.
evidence.summary: Supports the two British Library variant witnesses, their dates and physical contexts, exact loci, variant burdens, and the absence of linked digitized content in the current official records.

open_questions.question: What composition dates and original dialect regions can current scholarship support for each carol beyond Breul's broad fifteenth-century estimate and limited linguistic observations?
next_steps: Consult modern editions and linguistic studies of DIMEV records 1173 and 589.
Keep work composition, variant history, and each witness's copying date and place separate.
Do not infer an original North-Midland composition merely from the c.1480 NLS copy.

open_questions.question: What stemmatic or performance relationships connect the NLS texts with the shorter Cambridge, Bodley, Egerton, and Harley variants?
next_steps: Obtain complete images or reliable diplomatic transcriptions of the four collateral witnesses.
Compare textual and burden variants without presuming direct copying between extant manuscripts.
Consult modern carol and musicological editions for version-specific analysis.

open_questions.question: Should the two Chaucer epigraph lines currently encoded as L elements inside Annunciation stanza II be represented as explicit editorial paratext in future conversion or source cleanup?
next_steps: For cataloging and display, report 56 medieval Annunciation lines and identify the two extra L elements as editorial epigraph material.
Treat any XML structural correction as a separate source-repository change; do not silently alter the manifest count.

notes: The whole-unit form is mixed because the cataloged item is the complete article, not merely the two medieval poems.

Current NLS foliation and direct image boundaries control present-day locators; Breul's attributed historical folio statements are preserved in lineage notes, including the Nativity carol's discrepant fol.59vo fg.
Manuscript image and scan rights are not inferred from the public-domain status of the underlying medieval texts or nineteenth-century editions.
